itineraries.title: Complete El Salvador Road Trip: From the Country's Only UNESCO Site to the Morazán Mountains
itineraries.subtitle: 10 days from San Salvador to western volcanoes, the Pacific coast, coffee villages, and civil war memorials
itineraries.notes: Joya de Cerén is closed on Mondays—plan Day 2 for another day. The hike to Volcán Ilamatepec (Santa Ana) requires an official guide from Cerro Verde, morning departures only. El Mozote is visited from Perquín, 15 kilometers on partially unpaved road.

visits.text: Plaza Gerardo Barrios forms the historic heart of San Salvador. The Metropolitan Cathedral—consecrated in 1999 as the third iteration of this temple (the previous two destroyed by earthquakes and fire)—houses the tomb of Archbishop Óscar Romero, assassinated during Mass in 1980 and canonized in 2018. Across the square, step inside the Palacio Nacional: government headquarters until 1976, now a museum showcasing marble staircases, ornate chambers, and portraits of 19th-century presidents.

Just steps away, Iglesia El Rosario demands a visit. The unassuming brutalist concrete façade gives no hint of what's inside—then you enter: sculptor Rubén Martínez designed the plans in six days in 1962, with construction completed in 1971. The pillar-free arched structure is lined with stained glass that floods the nave with a rainbow of light. Come mid-morning for maximum illumination. Free entry.

Parque Cuscatlán contains the Memorial al Mártir y al Héroe Civil, a somber installation etching the names of thousands of civil war victims (1980-1992). It's a place of reflection frequented by Salvadoran families searching for a name on the stone wall.

activities.text: Dive into Mercado Central for 30 minutes before leaving: hot pupusas, tropical fruit, handicrafts, and cooking aromas. Stay alert around the market edges, especially late afternoon—mornings are safer.

map_keywords: Joya de Ceren archaeological site UNESCO
San Andres archaeological site Maya El Salvador

visits.text: Joya de Cerén is El Salvador's only UNESCO site and one of Central America's most extraordinary archaeological finds. In 600 CE, the Loma Caldera volcano buried this Maya farming village under 5 meters of ash—residents fled, leaving behind houses, kitchens, manioc gardens, and even a temazcal (steam bath), the only intact example ever found in the Maya world. Discovered accidentally in 1976 during silo construction, the site reveals the daily life of Maya peasants rather than kings—which makes it unique. Allow 90 minutes on the wooden walkways above the excavations. Closed Mondays.

Five kilometers from Joya de Cerén, San Andrés presents the complementary face of the same civilization: not peasant houses but the political and ceremonial center. Between 600 and 900 CE, San Andrés served as capital of a Maya polity controlling the Zapotitán valley, with an estimated population of 12,000. The Acropolis was constructed by filling an ancient plaza with more than 100,000 adobe blocks. The main pyramid (Structure 1) rises 15 meters. Trade connections with Copán (Honduras) and the Guatemalan Highlands have been proven through ceramics and pyrite mirrors found on site.

activities.text: Arriving in Santa Ana by evening, stroll around Parque Central: the neo-Gothic cathedral (built 1906-1954) and the Teatro Nacional (1910) face each other. Check the theater's schedule—performances are often pay-what-you-wish. Dine on the square, one of the country's most beautiful.

map_keywords: Santa Ana Volcano Ilamatepec Cerro Verde

visits.text: Volcán Ilamatepec (Santa Ana), at 2,381 meters, is El Salvador's highest peak and one of Central America's most spectacular hikes. At the summit, the crater reveals a sulfuric acid lake of surreal turquoise—the color shifts with the light. The last eruption in 2005 hurled car-sized boulders 1.5 kilometers. Mandatory guided departure from Cerro Verde starting at 8:30am. 7-kilometer round trip, 465 meters elevation gain, moderate difficulty. Arrive early—after 10am, clouds often obscure the crater.

On the descent, stop at the Tazumal site in Chalchuapa, 25 kilometers east. The main pyramid, 24 meters high, was occupied from 100 CE until the Spanish conquest—over 1,400 years of Pipil and Maya history. Allow an hour on site.

activities.text: Between the volcano and Tazumal, pause at Lake Coatepeque, a caldera filled with turquoise water at 745 meters elevation. The water stays around 25°C in dry season—swim from lakeside restaurant docks, or simply enjoy the view from the road above.

map_keywords: Concepcion de Ataco murals El Salvador
Ruta de las Flores El Salvador

visits.text: The Ruta de las Flores winds 35 kilometers through five villages between 700 and 1,225 meters elevation. Start with Nahuizalco, the least touristy: its candlelit night market (open until 10pm) has sold rattan and tule handicrafts for generations. The Nahuatl name means 'the four Izalcos'—the village was founded by four families from Izalco.

Concepción de Ataco is the route's most colorful village. Since 2004, when residents participated in the 'Pueblos Vivos' program, hundreds of murals cover the walls, telling Pipil history. Step into the workshops: weavers still work traditional treadle looms, producing hand-embroidered textiles and garments. The village was founded by Pipils in the heart of the Apaneca cordillera.

Apaneca, 5 kilometers from Ataco, is the highest village (1,225 meters) and the heart of the coffee region. The Bourbon (64%) and Pacas (26%) varieties, shade-grown on these volcanic soils, rank among the world's most prized coffees. A finca will show you the complete chain, from red cherry to cup.

activities.text: Operators in Apaneca offer guided tastings with artisanal roasting demonstrations. Allow 90 minutes. You can also combine this with a zipline above the coffee plants—local operators run a 420-meter course above the tropical forest.

map_keywords: Juayua waterfalls Los Chorros de la Calera
Juayua Ruta de las Flores Sonsonate

visits.text: Juayúa—'river of purple orchids' in Nahuatl—is the Ruta de las Flores' pivotal village. Its church houses the Cristo Negro, a Black Christ statue carved in the 16th century, object of an annual pilgrimage drawing thousands of faithful. Wander the parque central and side streets—this is the route's liveliest village, with authentic local life.

The Los Chorros de la Calera waterfalls lie 2 kilometers from downtown. Water tumbles in several successive falls into natural pools. Hire a guide from your accommodation—the path crosses isolated areas and some incidents have been reported in recent years. Allow 2 hours round trip.

activities.text: If you're there on a weekend, the Feria Gastronómica de Juayúa in the parque central is unmissable. Every Saturday and Sunday for over 20 years, dozens of stalls take over the square: gourmet pupusas, grilled shrimp, iguana, specialty coffee. Come hungry—portions are generous and prices ridiculously low.

map_keywords: El Tunco surf beach El Salvador
El Sunzal surf spot La Libertad
Walter Thilo Deininger National Park El Salvador

visits.text: El Tunco is the Salvadoran Pacific coast's surf hub. The black volcanic sand beach is dominated by a pig-shaped rock. The waves at El Sunzal, 1 kilometer away, host WSL and ISA competitions—watch the surfers from the rocky point before heading to bed: the evening spectacle, with silhouettes on the swells, is striking.

The seafood market in La Libertad, 5 kilometers east, is one of the country's most vibrant. Lobsters, shrimp, fresh mariscos—vendors cook directly on the port. Go early morning, before 9am, when boats return.

activities.text: Take a surf lesson in El Tunco—operators offer beginner sessions, board and instructor included. The waves are consistent, suitable for beginners, but currents can be strong. Follow instructions carefully. Allow 2 hours.

Walter Thilo Deininger National Park, 12 kilometers east, is Central America's oldest national park (founded 1921). A tropical dry forest sheltering deer, coatis, and iguanas. The main trail takes 2 hours.

map_keywords: El Zonte Beach Bitcoin Beach El Salvador
El Zonte surf El Salvador Pacific
Tamanique Waterfalls La Libertad El Salvador

visits.text: El Zonte, 5 kilometers west of El Tunco, is calmer and more family-friendly. Nicknamed 'Bitcoin Beach' since 2019, when an anonymous donor launched a Bitcoin ecosystem in this fishing village—the experiment directly inspired the 2021 law making Bitcoin legal tender in El Salvador. Between 2015 and 2024, the price per square meter jumped 134.8%. Swim, watch surfers from the river mouth, or explore the sea caves under cliffs accessible at low tide.

The coastal walk from El Zonte to El Tunco on foot (5 kilometers, 40 minutes) follows black volcanic cliffs, passing rock formations and isolated coves. Leave at low tide to access rocky passages. It's one of the country's wildest coastal walks.

activities.text: The Tamanique waterfalls lie 15 kilometers inland—several falls into natural pools surrounded by dense tropical forest. Local operators offer transfers from El Zonte. Allow 2.5 hours including travel.
From July to November, community programs organize nighttime baby sea turtle releases. Nestings occur at night and releases at dawn. Ask your accommodation for dates.

map_keywords: Alegria Lagoon Tecapa volcano El Salvador
Alegria flower village Usulutan El Salvador

visits.text: Alegría, at 1,250 meters elevation in the Usulután sierra, is nicknamed the 'Garden of El Salvador' for its tropical flower nurseries. Chilean poet Gabriela Mistral (1945 Nobel Prize for Literature) baptized its volcanic lagoon 'the Emerald of the Americas.' Stroll the white cobblestone streets—the pace here is deliberately slow. The village takes its name from a 19th-century Latin professor, Presbyter José Miguel Alegría, whose remains rest in the crypt of the main church.

Laguna de Alegría sits 2 kilometers from downtown, in the crater of Volcán Tecapa (1,603 meters). Its emerald-green sulfurous waters—impossible to swim in, the acid is too concentrated—bubble in places: active fumaroles heat the water from below. Circle the crater on foot (1.5 kilometers, slippery terrain in spots). Locals collect the mineral-rich clay from the banks, using it as a cosmetic treatment.

activities.text: The Mirador de las Cien Gradas ('hundred steps viewpoint'), above the village, offers panoramas across the Tecapa sierra and the coastal plain to the Pacific on clear days. Climb late afternoon for golden light on the coffee plantations below.

map_keywords: Museum of the Salvadoran Revolution Perquin
El Mozote massacre memorial El Salvador

visits.text: The Museo de la Revolución Salvadoreña in Perquín is one of Central America's most moving museums. The guides are former FMLN guerrillas—they show you the downed helicopter that killed Colonel Domingo Monterrosa in 1984, the clandestine radios of Radio Venceremos, the guerrillas' improvised weapons. A living memorial to the 12-year civil war (1980-1992) that left 75,000 dead and 8,000 disappeared.

El Mozote, 15 kilometers from Perquín, is an essential memorial site. On December 11, 1981, the Atlácatl Battalion killed 978 civilians—220 men, 200 women, 541 children, most under 12 years old. The memorial lists each name by family. Descendants of the victims will greet you on site. A criminal trial was initiated in late 2025.

activities.text: The Morazán mountains around Perquín conceal waterfalls in a pine forest at 1,200 meters elevation. Local guides organize 3-4 hour hikes to La Olomina and Las Pilas. The cool climate (often below 18°C) contrasts sharply with the rest of the country.

map_keywords: Los Tercios Waterfall Suchitoto basalt columns

visits.text: Suchitoto is often called El Salvador's most beautiful town. Its cobblestone streets, pastel adobe colonial houses, and Santa Lucía church—built in 1853 on the ruins of a 17th-century straw temple—make it feel timeless. Founded in 1528 by the Spanish as the country's first capital, it grew wealthy on indigo, nicknamed 'blue gold.' Get lost in the side streets in the morning, before the weekend influx of local visitors.

Los Tercios waterfall sits 1 kilometer from downtown in the quebrada Las Ánimas. What makes it unique: a vertical wall of perfectly aligned hexagonal basalt columns—10 meters high, as if hand-carved. It flows during rainy season (June-November); in dry season, the geological formation alone justifies the trip. Three miradors also offer views over Lake Suchitlán.

activities.text: Lake Suchitlán, created in 1976 by the Cerrón Grande dam, spans 135 square kilometers. Take a boat from San Juan port to Isla de los Pájaros—a sanctuary with over 170 recorded bird species—and Isla del Ermitaño. Allow 90 minutes on the water. Leave early morning for birdwatching.

Late afternoon before returning to San Salvador (50 kilometers, 1 hour), climb to the miradors overlooking the lake from downtown. Sunset over Suchitlán from the colonial house terraces is one of El Salvador's most photographed scenes.

seo.h1_title: Complete El Salvador Road Trip: From the Country's Only UNESCO Site to the Morazán Mountains
seo.intro_paragraph: In 10 days, this road trip crosses El Salvador west to east without compromise: Joya de Cerén (the country's only UNESCO site, the 'Pompeii of the Americas'), the turquoise crater of Volcán Ilamatepec at 2,381 meters, two nights on the Pacific coast between surf and Bitcoin Beach, the emerald lagoon of Alegría baptized by Gabriela Mistral, then the El Mozote memorial and the colonial streets of Suchitoto. No drive exceeds 2 hours.
seo.seo_hook: The country's only UNESCO site buried under volcanic ash, turquoise craters, Pacific swells, and traces of civil war—El Salvador from end to end without compromise.
featured_list: Joya de Cerén: El Salvador's only UNESCO site, a Maya village buried in 600 CE
Volcán Ilamatepec: sulfurous turquoise crater lake at 2,381 meters, last erupted 2005
Alegría: emerald lagoon at the bottom of Volcán Tecapa, nicknamed 'Emerald of the Americas'
El Mozote: memorial to the 978 victims of the December 11, 1981 massacre

places_taglines.SV::san-salvador: Capital of 3 million with El Rosario church and its rainbow stained glass
places_taglines.SV::santa-ana: The country's second city, gateway to El Salvador's highest volcano and its crater lake
places_taglines.SV::concepcion-de-ataco: Pipil village at 1,225 meters whose walls have been covered in murals since 2004
places_taglines.SV::juayua: Ruta de las Flores village with its Sunday food fair running for 20 years
places_taglines.SV::el-tunco: Surf City's surf capital, hosting WSL competitions on its consistent waves
places_taglines.SV::el-zonte: Bitcoin Beach, the fishing village that inspired El Salvador's 2021 crypto law
places_taglines.SV::alegria: Flower village at 1,250 meters whose emerald lagoon was named by Gabriela Mistral
places_taglines.SV::perquin: Former FMLN stronghold, heart of the Peace Route and civil war memory
places_taglines.SV::suchitoto: First Spanish capital of the country in 1528, enriched by indigo called 'blue gold'

seo_keywords: joya de ceren UNESCO el salvador
ruta de las flores el salvador
san andres maya site el salvador

highlights: Joya de Cerén, El Salvador's only UNESCO site, inscribed in 1993: a Maya village buried around 600 CE under 5 meters of ash—the 'Pompeii of the Americas,' discovered accidentally in 1976 during silo construction

Volcán Ilamatepec (Santa Ana), 2,381 meters, the country's highest peak—its turquoise sulfuric crater lake is visible from the rim, without barriers, surrounded by active fumaroles since the last eruption in 2005
The Ruta de las Flores: 35 kilometers of scenic road connecting 5 villages between 700 and 1,225 meters—Juayúa's Feria Gastronómica every weekend for over 20 years
El Mozote: on December 11, 1981, the Atlácatl Battalion massacred 978 civilians including 541 children—the memorial lists each name; a trial is underway since 2025

Alegría and its Laguna de Alegría, nicknamed 'the Emerald of the Americas' by Chilean poet Gabriela Mistral (1945 Nobel Prize)—sulfurous emerald-green water at the bottom of Volcán Tecapa crater (1,603 meters)

itineraries.title: Essential El Salvador Circuit: Flower Route and the Pacific
itineraries.subtitle: San Salvador, Santa Ana volcanoes, coffee villages, and surf beaches in one week
itineraries.notes: The Ilamatepec volcano hike requires an official guide from Cerro Verde, with mandatory morning departures. Days 6-7 at El Zonte end just 60 km from San Salvador airport - an easy return on the final day.

visits.text: Start at Plaza Gerardo Barrios, the historic heart of San Salvador. The Metropolitan Cathedral - consecrated in 1999, the third version of the temple (the previous two destroyed by earthquakes) - houses the tomb of Archbishop Óscar Romero, assassinated in 1980 and canonized in 2018. Next door, the National Palace, seat of government until 1976, has become a museum: marble staircases, ornate rooms, colonial style at its peak. Modest entrance fee.

El Rosario Church, facing Plaza Libertad, is one of the most surprising religious buildings in Central America. From outside, an uninviting raw concrete facade. Step inside: architect-sculptor Rubén Martínez designed the plans in 1962, completed in 1971 - a pillarless arched structure whose ceiling is lined with stained glass that floods the nave with a rainbow of light. Come in mid-morning or late afternoon for maximum light. Free entry.

Parque Cuscatlán, a few minutes' walk away, houses the Memorial to the Martyr and Civil Hero - a sober installation listing the names of thousands of victims of the civil war (1980-1992). It's a place of remembrance, often visited by families searching for a name on the stone wall.

Before leaving downtown, spend 30 minutes diving into the Central Market. Loud, colorful, dense - you'll find hot pupusas, exotic fruits, and handicrafts at every price point. Stay alert in the immediate market surroundings, especially late afternoon.

activities.text: To understand the city as a whole, local operators offer 3-4 hour walking tours of the historic center: colonial architecture, civil war stories, street food, anecdotes about everyday Salvadoran life. Book the day before - groups depart early morning to beat the heat.

practical_info.langue: Spanish or English depending on operator

map_keywords: Santa Ana Volcano Ilamatepec Cerro Verde

visits.text: At the summit of Ilamatepec volcano (2,381 m, El Salvador's highest), expect something extraordinary: a sulfurous turquoise crater lake almost surreal in color, surrounded by fumaroles spewing clouds of sulfur. The hike - 7 km round trip, 465 m elevation gain, moderate level - starts from Cerro Verde park with a mandatory guide from 8:30 AM. Take a private guide rather than the group if you want to move at your own pace. Arrive before 9 AM to beat the clouds.

Descending from the volcano, a cliffside road passes above Lake Coatepeque - a volcanic caldera filled with turquoise water at 745 m altitude. Stop at the viewpoints along the road: the plunging view of the lake with Izalco volcano in the background is one of the country's most photographed panoramas.

activities.text: Cerro Verde park rangers organize naturalist outings in the cloud forest between Izalco and Santa Ana: hummingbirds, quetzals (rare but possible), bromeliads, orchids. An hour-long guided walk through the park before tackling the volcano ascent makes for a good warm-up.

map_keywords: Concepcion de Ataco murals El Salvador
Ruta de las Flores El Salvador villages

visits.text: The Ruta de las Flores connects 5 villages over 35 km between 700 and 1,225 m elevation - the name comes from wildflowers that carpet the hills from November to February. Start with the lowest, Nahuizalco: the Nahuatl name means 'the 4 Izalcos'. Its candlelit night market (until 10 PM) is one of the region's most authentic experiences - wicker and tule handicrafts, tourist-free.

Concepción de Ataco is the most colorful and most photographed village on the route. Since 2004 - when residents participated in the government's 'Pueblos Vivos' program - hundreds of murals cover the walls, telling the story of Pipil history and Salvadoran culture. Step into the weavers' workshops: foot-powered looms still operate to produce hand-embroidered textiles and clothing.

Apaneca, 5 km from Ataco, is the highest village (1,225 m) and the heart of the coffee region. A finca will show you the complete chain - from hand-picked red cherry to cup. The Bourbon and Pacas varieties grown under shade on these volcanic soils are among the world's most prized.

activities.text: Operators in Apaneca offer guided coffee tastings with demonstration of the complete process: sorting, pulping, fermentation, drying, artisan roasting, grinding. Allow 1h30. You'll leave with a better understanding of what's happening in your cup - and often a bag of local coffee.

map_keywords: Juayua waterfalls Los Chorros de la Calera
Juayua Ruta de las Flores Sonsonate

visits.text: Juayúa - 'river of purple orchids' in Nahuatl - is the main village of the Ruta de las Flores. Its church houses the Cristo Negro, a Black Christ statue carved in the 16th century and object of an annual pilgrimage. Wander around the central park and surrounding streets - the village is livelier than Ataco and more popular with backpackers, but retains its charm.

Los Chorros de la Calera waterfalls are 2 km from downtown Juayúa, below the road. Water tumbles in several successive falls into natural pools where you can swim. Take a guide from your accommodation - the path crosses isolated areas and some incidents have been reported. Allow 2h round trip.

activities.text: If you're there on a weekend, the Feria Gastronómica de Juayúa on the central park is unmissable. Every Saturday and Sunday for over 20 years, dozens of stalls offer gourmet pupusas, grilled shrimp, iguana, rabbit, smoked meats, and specialty coffee. Come hungry - portions are generous. On weekdays, the artisan market around the plaza is also worth an hour's visit.

map_keywords: El Tunco surf beach El Salvador
El Sunzal surf spot La Libertad
Playa El Tunco La Libertad El Salvador

visits.text: Welcome to Surf City: El Tunco is the surf capital of El Salvador's Pacific coast, 65 km west of San Salvador. The black volcanic sand beach is dominated by a pig-shaped rock - 'tunco' in local slang. The waves at El Sunzal, 1 km away, host international WSL and ISA competitions: watch the locals surf from the point, even if you don't surf yourself.

El Tunco's central rock hides sea caves accessible on foot at low tide. Go explore them before 10 AM, when the tide comes back in. The black volcanic formations contrast with the Pacific's white foam - it's photographically spectacular.

activities.text: Operators in El Tunco offer beginner surf lessons, board and instructor included. The waves are consistent and moderate in height - ideal for learning. Currents can be strong: always follow instructions. 2-hour session.

map_keywords: Playa El Zonte Bitcoin Beach El Salvador
El Zonte surf El Salvador Pacific
Tamanique Waterfalls La Libertad El Salvador

visits.text: El Zonte, 5 km west of El Tunco, is quieter and more family-friendly. In 2019, an anonymous donor launched a Bitcoin ecosystem in this fishing village - merchants began accepting crypto, and this experiment directly inspired the 2021 law making Bitcoin legal tender in El Salvador. Stroll along the black coastline, swim in the rollers, or watch surfers from the river mouth.

Sunset from El Zonte's river mouth, where fresh and salt water meet on black sand, is one of El Salvador's most peaceful moments. Settle in there by 5:30 PM - the orange light on the waves lasts less than an hour.

activities.text: From July to November, community programs organize nighttime baby sea turtle releases on La Libertad's beaches. Check with your accommodation for exact dates - nestings happen at night and releases take place at dawn. A rare experience.

Tamanique waterfalls are 15 km inland, accessible by car then on foot (30 min trail). Several successive falls drop into natural pools surrounded by dense tropical vegetation. Local operators offer tours by tuktuk or car from El Zonte.

map_keywords: Playa El Zonte Bitcoin Beach El Salvador
El Tunco El Zonte coastal walk

visits.text: Final morning: enjoy El Zonte beach before departure. The black volcanic sand retains heat from 7 AM onward. If you surf, this is the moment - morning waves are generally cleaner and less crowded than during the day.

activities.text: Take the coastal walk from El Zonte to El Tunco on foot (40 min, 5 km). The path follows volcanic cliffs, passes wave-sculpted rock formations, and crosses a few isolated coves. Go at low tide to access the rocky passages. San Salvador airport is 60 km away - allow 1h30 driving at day's end.

seo.h1_title: Essential El Salvador Circuit: Flower Route and the Pacific
seo.intro_paragraph: In 7 days, this circuit distills the essence of El Salvador: the turquoise crater of Ilamatepec volcano (2,381 m), the coffee and art villages of the Ruta de las Flores, and the Pacific coast at El Tunco and El Zonte - aka Bitcoin Beach, the country's first crypto ecosystem. A balanced rhythm between mountains, villages, and beaches.
seo.seo_hook: Active volcanoes, coffee villages with fresco-covered walls, and Pacific waves on black volcanic sand - one week through El Salvador end to end.
featured_list: Ilamatepec Volcano: sulfurous turquoise crater lake at 2,381 m elevation
Concepción de Ataco: Ruta de las Flores village covered in murals since 2004
Juayúa: weekend Feria Gastronómica for 20+ years on the central park
El Zonte: Bitcoin Beach, birthplace of crypto adoption in El Salvador

places_taglines.SV::san-salvador: Capital at the foot of a volcano, home to El Rosario Church and its rainbow stained glass
places_taglines.SV::santa-ana: Gateway to the country's highest volcano and its turquoise crater lake
places_taglines.SV::concepcion-de-ataco: Pipil village at 1,225 m covered in murals telling Salvadoran history
places_taglines.SV::juayua: Ruta de las Flores village famous for its Sunday Feria Gastronómica
places_taglines.SV::el-tunco: Surf City village hosting WSL competitions on its consistent waves
places_taglines.SV::el-zonte: Bitcoin Beach, the fishing village that inspired El Salvador's 2021 crypto law

seo_keywords: ruta de las flores el salvador

highlights: The Ruta de las Flores connects 5 villages over 35 km between 700 and 1,225 m elevation - wildflowers carpet the hills from November to February
Ilamatepec volcano (Santa Ana) peaks at 2,381 m, the country's highest - its sulfurous turquoise crater lake is visible from the crater rim, with no safety barrier
El Rosario Church in San Salvador, completed in 1971, conceals behind a raw concrete facade a cascade of rainbow stained glass designed in 6 days by sculptor Rubén Martínez
El Zonte, nicknamed 'Bitcoin Beach', was El Salvador's first Bitcoin ecosystem in 2019 - this village directly inspired the law making Bitcoin legal tender in the country in 2021
The Feria Gastronómica de Juayúa has taken place every weekend for over 20 years on the central park - pupusas, seafood, specialty coffee
